entity: University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill
summary: I came to the University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill around 1900 as a student and stayed to graduate. It was here that my interest in writing and journalism took root, which eventually led me to a career in newspapers. Years later, after my time abroad, I was invited to join the faculty here, thanks in part to Frank Porter Graham's encouragement. I've been part of the university community for about forty years now.

I never actually attended the London School of Economics, but I was in London during the early 1920s when Frank Porter Graham was studying there. I met him several times and we had some long conversations, which influenced my later decision to join the UNC faculty. Although I didn't study at LSE, my time in London overlapped with his, and that connection was important for my career.

In the early 1920s, I worked for the London Daily Express and was the only American on their staff. Initially, I covered various topics, but later I became an 'idea man' to help promote and publicize the paper. The owner, Lord Beaverbrook, was very interested in American ideas and encouraged me in this role. I stayed with the paper for nearly five years before returning to the U.S. in 1925.

Lord Beaverbrook was the Canadian owner of the London Daily Express when I worked there in the early 1920s. He had a keen interest in American ways and was instrumental in pushing me into a role where I could promote and publicize the paper using American ideas. His influence helped shape my work in London and gave me a unique position at the paper.

I met Frank Porter Graham in London during the time he was studying at the London School of Economics in the early 1920s. We had several long talks, and he later became convinced that I belonged on the faculty at UNC Chapel Hill. Thanks to his support, I was invited to join the university, and I've been here ever since. Our relationship was pivotal in my academic career.

I remember Lois MacDonald vaguely from the time when she was in London, around the same time as Frank Porter Graham. I didn't make a special effort to meet other Americans there because I wanted to immerse myself fully in English life. So while I recall her, I didn't really cultivate a relationship with her or other Americans during that period.

When Frank Graham asked me about the trade union school at Black Mountain, I wasn't sure if it was the same as the Southern Summer School. The trade union school was backed by the Textile Workers Union and focused more strictly on labor issues. I had some involvement or contact with it, but I don't recall all the details clearly as it has been a long time.

The Textile Workers Union supported the trade union school at Black Mountain by sending organizers to check on progress from time to time. This school was more focused on labor issues compared to the Southern Summer School. My involvement with the union school gave me insight into labor organizing and education during that era.

I spent one summer at the Southern Summer School in Asheville, North Carolina, on the grounds of Black Mountain College. Mrs. McLaren was the director, and I remember her as a large, gentle southern matron with whom I got along well. The Southern Summer School was different from the textile workers' trade union school and had a broader educational focus.

I spent a summer in Asheville, North Carolina, attending the Southern Summer School held on the grounds of Black Mountain College. It was during this time that I got to know Mrs. McLaren, the school's director. Asheville served as a backdrop for this educational experience focused on labor and social issues.

One of Mrs. McLaren's associates at the Southern Summer School later became editor of the Monthly Review, a liberal and labor magazine based in New York. I attended some of his lectures on economics but didn't have much contact with him outside the classroom. The Monthly Review represented a political activist perspective that was common among the school's assistants.

Leo Huberman was one of the associates at the Southern Summer School who taught economics and later became editor of the Monthly Review in New York. I saw him mostly during lectures and didn't have much interaction outside of class. While we didn't have much in common personally, he was clearly a political activist focused on labor issues.

Mildred Price was one of Mrs. McLaren's associates at the Southern Summer School, and I believe she taught English. I mostly saw her in the classroom and didn't interact much outside of that. Like Huberman, she was politically active and part of the group that emphasized political action in the labor movement.

I traveled around the country with Big Bill Haywood, the labor agitator, as I was working on his biography. He often stayed at my New York apartment when he needed to hide. Unfortunately, he was indicted by the U.S. government for his anti-war stance and sent to Russia, and I never saw him again. His departure was a big disappointment because I hoped to document his life and the labor movement through him.

Big Bill Haywood was associated with the Industrial Workers of the World, and when he was indicted along with some IWW leaders for anti-war agitation, he was sent to Russia. This was a significant moment in the labor movement and affected my work with him. The IWW represented a more radical labor faction during that time.

After Big Bill Haywood was indicted for his anti-war activities, he was sent to Russia and I never saw him again. We had hoped that his visit to Russia would calm tensions and that he would return to continue his work in the labor movement. His absence left me feeling let down, as I wanted to write his biography and document labor history.

During my time at the Southern Summer School and in the labor movement, there were two schools of thought about labor's direction. One side, which included the American Federation of Labor, focused on political action like voting and elections. I felt that this was not enough and that strong economic action was equally necessary for labor's progress.

The New Deal was a political program that came about during the late 1930s, and while many saw it as a way for labor to gain power, I considered it purely political action. I believed that economic action, such as strikes and direct labor efforts, was more crucial than just voting or supporting political candidates. The New Deal didn't fully address the economic strength labor needed.

The Lawrence textile strike in Lawrence, Massachusetts, was one of the few strikes controlled by Big Bill Haywood. During that strike, he managed to get the children sent away for their safety. This strike was an example of the kind of strong economic action I believed was necessary for labor, beyond just political efforts.

Lawrence, Massachusetts, was the site of a major textile strike that Big Bill Haywood helped lead. The strike was notable for Haywood's efforts to protect the children by sending them away during the unrest. This event was a significant example of labor activism and direct economic action during that period.
